refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and the system to achieve and maintain energy efficiency performance such that measured energy_per_ton (energy_used / ton_moved) does not exceed the customer-defined E_target over the agreed evaluation period, under nominal operating conditions and across representative worksite scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-003 is refined to be measurable, time-bound, and scenario-aware. It explicitly ties the functional requirement to the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) and acknowledges that energy efficiency is a system-level property dependent on operator behavior, vehicle design, and operational context. The refinement separates the capability (enable achievement) from the outcome (measured performance), allowing decomposition into sub-requirements addressing sensing, control, feedback, and operational guidance.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure energy_per_ton across a defined evaluation period covering multiple representative worksite scenarios (e.g., load types, terrain, operator skill levels). Compare measured value against E_target. Verify that the NGHE solution's design, telemetry, and operational guidance contribute measurably to achieving the target.
sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The NGHE solution shall acquire and report real-time energy consumption data (fuel/power input) with sufficient accuracy and frequency to enable energy efficiency monitoring.
FR-EE-002: The NGHE solution shall acquire and report real-time productivity data (ton_moved, work cycles, idle time) with sufficient accuracy to enable energy efficiency calculation.
FR-EE-003: The NGHE solution shall compute and display energy efficiency metrics (energy_per_ton) to the operator in real-time to support operator decision-making.
FR-EE-004: The NGHE solution shall provide operational guidance (via teleoperation or autonomous logic) to optimize energy efficiency without compromising safety or productivity.
FR-EE-005: The NGHE solution shall log and aggregate energy efficiency data over the evaluation period to support post-evaluation analysis and reporting.

candidate_dps: DP-EE-A: Implement a closed-loop energy optimization system comprising real-time energy and productivity sensing, on-board computation of efficiency metrics, operator feedback via HMI, and adaptive control logic that adjusts vehicle operating parameters (engine throttle, hydraulic flow, transmission mode) to minimize energy_per_ton while respecting safety and productivity constraints.

DP-EE-B: Implement a passive telemetry and reporting system that logs energy and productivity data for post-hoc analysis, with operator guidance provided via periodic reports and recommendations from a remote tele-operator or analytics service.

DP-EE-C: Implement a hybrid approach combining real-time on-board efficiency monitoring and operator feedback (DP-EE-A elements) with remote tele-operator oversight and advisory guidance (DP-EE-B elements), enabling both autonomous optimization and human-in-the-loop refinement.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-EE-C: Hybrid real-time on-board energy efficiency monitoring and optimization with remote tele-operator advisory oversight. This DP combines continuous on-board sensing, computation, and feedback to enable the vehicle to operate efficiently autonomously, while leveraging the Teleoperation module to provide expert guidance, anomaly detection, and adaptive strategy refinement based on worksite conditions and historical performance data.

potential_sources: Energy sensing (fuel flow, electrical power) is decoupled from productivity sensing (load cell, cycle counters) via independent sensor streams and data fusion logic.
On-board efficiency computation is decoupled from remote advisory logic via asynchronous telemetry and messaging (Telemetry interface).
Operator feedback (HMI display) is decoupled from control actuation (engine/hydraulic adjustments) via a decision layer that respects safety constraints.
Tele-operator guidance is advisory and does not override safety-critical vehicle control, maintaining clear separation of concerns.
coupling_check.notes: DP-EE-C exhibits low coupling because energy optimization logic is modular: sensing, computation, feedback, and remote advisory are distinct functional layers with well-defined interfaces. The hybrid approach avoids tight coupling between on-board and remote systems by using asynchronous telemetry and advisory messaging rather than synchronous remote control.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Energy sensors (fuel flow, electrical power) are available on the vehicle platform with sufficient accuracy (±5% or better) to enable meaningful efficiency metrics.
Assumption: Productivity sensors (load cells, cycle counters, GPS) are available and integrated into the vehicle's data acquisition system.
Assumption: Network connectivity (Telemetry interface) is reliable enough to support near-real-time advisory guidance without introducing safety-critical latency.
Risk: If energy or productivity sensors are unavailable or inaccurate, efficiency metrics will be unreliable, undermining operator confidence and decision-making.
Risk: If network latency is high or connectivity is intermittent, remote advisory guidance may become stale or unavailable, reducing the value of the hybrid approach.
Risk: Operator acceptance of efficiency guidance may be low if it conflicts with perceived productivity or safety priorities; cultural and training factors are critical.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify sensor availability and accuracy requirements in the Vehicle module specification. Define network latency and availability SLAs for the Telemetry interface to ensure advisory guidance is timely. Establish operator training and change management protocols to ensure adoption of efficiency-focused operational practices.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system boundary encompassing all subsystems and services required to achieve energy efficiency. It integrates the Vehicle (on-board sensing, computation, and control), Teleoperation (remote advisory and oversight), and Safety Services (constraint enforcement) to deliver the energy efficiency capability. The NGHE Solution is responsible for orchestrating data flow, ensuring consistency of efficiency metrics, and coordinating between on-board and remote optimization logic.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional telemetry stream carrying real-time energy consumption, productivity, and efficiency metrics from Vehicle to Teleoperation and advisory guidance from Teleoperation back to Vehicle. Supports near-real-time monitoring and remote advisory.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Worksite task assignments and operational parameters (e.g., load targets, cycle time constraints, terrain type) provided by Site Manager or autonomous planning system. Informs energy optimization strategy.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety-critical alerts and constraints from Safety Services. Ensures energy optimization does not compromise safety; energy optimization logic must respect safety constraints enforced via SafetyAlert.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network connectivity enabling Telemetry and advisory communication between on-board Vehicle systems and remote Teleoperation services.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level container for energy efficiency capability. All sub-components (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) are logically contained within the NGHE Solution boundary. Minimal diffusion: only external interfaces (Tasking, Network Link) cross the boundary. This is the correct architectural choice.

Vehicle module contains on-board sensing, computation, and control logic for energy efficiency. However, remote advisory guidance (Teleoperation) and safety constraint enforcement (Safety Services) are external to Vehicle, creating diffusion. Vehicle alone cannot fully realize DP-EE-C without external coordination.

Teleoperation module provides remote advisory and oversight but depends on Vehicle telemetry and on-board control actuation. Energy efficiency optimization logic is split between on-board (Vehicle) and remote (Teleoperation) layers, creating diffusion and coordination complexity.

Safety Services enforces constraints but does not directly implement energy efficiency optimization. Energy efficiency logic must integrate with Safety Services to respect constraints, creating bidirectional diffusion.

assigned_im.ownership_candidate: NGHE Solution (System-level ownership). Energy efficiency is a system-level property that emerges from the coordinated interaction of Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. No single module can own energy efficiency in isolation; ownership must reside at the NGHE Solution level with clear delegation of sub-responsibilities to Vehicle (on-board optimization), Teleoperation (remote advisory), and Safety Services (constraint enforcement).
assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Energy efficiency capability is best realized by assigning the DP-EE-C implementation to the NGHE Solution module, which orchestrates contributions from Vehicle (on-board sensing and control), Teleoperation (remote advisory), and Safety Services (constraint enforcement). This avoids artificial containment within a single subsystem and acknowledges the distributed, multi-agent nature of energy optimization.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution is treated as a passive container rather than an active orchestrator, coordination between Vehicle and Teleoperation may be loose, leading to inconsistent efficiency metrics or conflicting guidance.
Risk: Ownership ambiguity between Vehicle and Teleoperation regarding who is responsible for energy optimization decisions could lead to finger-pointing and delayed issue resolution.

Risk: If Safety Services constraints are not tightly integrated into the energy optimization logic, safety-critical scenarios may be missed, or energy optimization may inadvertently compromise safety.
Risk: Diffusion of energy efficiency logic across multiple modules increases testing and integration complexity; end-to-end verification of energy efficiency may be difficult.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Establish explicit ownership and coordination protocols at the NGHE Solution level. Define clear interfaces and responsibilities: Vehicle owns on-board sensing and real-time efficiency computation; Teleoperation owns remote advisory and strategy refinement; Safety Services owns constraint enforcement. Create a system-level energy efficiency orchestration layer (e.g., a coordinator component or service) that manages data flow and decision-making across these modules. Document the end-to-end energy efficiency workflow and verification strategy.

fr_dp_gate.reason: The refined FR (enable achievement of energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly addressed by DP-EE-C (hybrid on-board and remote energy optimization). The DP specifies a concrete solution approach (real-time sensing, computation, feedback, and remote advisory) that logically satisfies the FR. The DP is implementable and testable. No fundamental mismatch between problem and solution.

diffusion_risks: Energy efficiency logic is distributed across Vehicle (on-board), Teleoperation (remote), and Safety Services (constraints). This is intentional and necessary but increases integration complexity.
Telemetry interface is critical for DP-EE-C; if Telemetry is unavailable or unreliable, the hybrid approach fails. Network Link dependency is a single point of failure.
Coordination between on-board and remote optimization logic requires careful synchronization; race conditions or stale data could lead to suboptimal or unsafe decisions.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary because energy efficiency is a system-level property. Assigning DP-EE-C to NGHE Solution acknowledges that no single subsystem (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) can independently realize energy efficiency; the capability emerges from their coordinated interaction. This is architecturally sound but requires explicit orchestration and clear interface contracts.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Energy efficiency can be verified by measuring energy_per_ton over the evaluation period and comparing against E_target. Verification requires integration of Vehicle (sensing), Teleoperation (advisory), and Safety Services (constraints). End-to-end testing is feasible but requires coordinated test scenarios covering multiple worksite conditions and operator behaviors.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns the overall energy efficiency capability; Vehicle owns on-board sensing and control; Teleoperation owns remote advisory; Safety Services owns constraint enforcement. Sub-FRs (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-005) can be allocated to specific modules in the next decomposition level.

recommended_revisions: Establish a system-level energy efficiency orchestration layer or coordinator component within NGHE Solution to manage data flow and decision-making across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services.
Define explicit interface contracts for Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link to ensure consistent data semantics and timing across modules.
Develop a detailed end-to-end energy efficiency workflow diagram showing data flow, decision points, and feedback loops.
Specify network latency and availability SLAs for Telemetry and Network Link to ensure remote advisory guidance is timely and reliable.
Plan for operator training and change management to ensure adoption of efficiency-focused operational practices.
Identify and mitigate single points of failure (e.g., Network Link, Telemetry interface) through redundancy or fallback strategies.

decision.justification: The refined FR is clear, the selected DP is sound and implementable, and the assigned IM (NGHE Solution) is architecturally appropriate. However, the DP-EE-C is complex and distributed across multiple modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services), requiring further decomposition to clarify sub-responsibilities and interfaces. The five sub-FRs (FR-EE-001 through FR-EE-005) are well-defined and ready for the next decomposition level. Proceeding to decompose sub-FRs will clarify module-level responsibilities and reduce integration risk.
decision.justification_detail: ZIGZAG_DOWN is appropriate because: (1) The current level (CR-NGHE-003) has been successfully refined and allocated to NGHE Solution with clear sub-FRs. (2) The sub-FRs are atomic and ready for further decomposition into module-specific DPs and IMs. (3) Decomposing sub-FRs will clarify how Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services contribute to energy efficiency, reducing architectural ambiguity. (4) No fundamental issues with the current FR, DP, or IM require revision before proceeding.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-EE-001: The NGHE solution shall acquire and report real-time energy consumption data (fuel/power input) with sufficient accuracy and frequency to enable energy efficiency monitoring.
FR-EE-002: The NGHE solution shall acquire and report real-time productivity data (ton_moved, work cycles, idle time) with sufficient accuracy to enable energy efficiency calculation.
FR-EE-003: The NGHE solution shall compute and display energy efficiency metrics (energy_per_ton) to the operator in real-time to support operator decision-making.
FR-EE-004: The NGHE solution shall provide operational guidance (via teleoperation or autonomous logic) to optimize energy efficiency without compromising safety or productivity.
FR-EE-005: The NGHE solution shall log and aggregate energy efficiency data over the evaluation period to support post-evaluation analysis and reporting.

assumptions: Energy sensors (fuel flow, electrical power) are available on the vehicle platform with ±5% or better accuracy.
Productivity sensors (load cells, cycle counters, GPS) are integrated into the vehicle's data acquisition system.
Network connectivity (Telemetry interface) is reliable enough to support near-real-time advisory guidance with acceptable latency (<5 seconds for non-safety-critical guidance).
Operators are willing and able to adopt efficiency-focused operational practices with appropriate training and incentives.
Safety constraints (from Safety Services) can be expressed as optimization constraints that energy optimization logic can respect.
The evaluation period and representative worksite scenarios are well-defined and agreed upon with the customer.
open_risks: Sensor availability and accuracy: If energy or productivity sensors are unavailable or inaccurate, efficiency metrics will be unreliable.
Network reliability: If Telemetry or Network Link is unavailable or high-latency, remote advisory guidance may be stale or ineffective.
Operator adoption: If operators do not trust or adopt efficiency guidance, the system will not achieve the energy efficiency target.
Safety-efficiency trade-offs: Energy optimization logic may inadvertently compromise safety if constraints are not tightly integrated.
Integration complexity: Distributed energy efficiency logic across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services increases testing and integration risk.
Single points of failure: Network Link and Telemetry interface are critical; redundancy or fallback strategies may be needed.
evidence_needed: Sensor specifications and accuracy data for energy and productivity sensors.
Network latency and availability data for Telemetry and Network Link under typical worksite conditions.
Operator feedback and acceptance data from pilot testing or similar systems.
Safety constraint specifications and integration requirements from Safety Services.
End-to-end energy efficiency workflow and verification strategy.
Detailed module interface contracts for Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, and Network Link.
Test scenarios and acceptance criteria for energy efficiency verification.
